{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"span":4,"markdown":"Reporting completeness for SBA indicators in 2025 is generally high across most regions, showing improvement compared to earlier years. 

Among regions with observed disruptions (Ayn, Bakool, Bari, Bay, Benadir, Gedo, Hiiraan, Mudug (Galmudug), and Mudug (Puntland)):

- Low completeness (<80%) was observed in Ayn and Gedo, which may partially contribute to the apparent disruptions there.

- Moderate completeness (80–90%) occurred in Bakool, Bay, Benadir, and Gedo, indicating some fluctuations that could slightly influence interpretation of disruptions in those regions.

- The remaining regions maintained consistently high completeness (≥90%), suggesting that disruptions observed there are unlikely to be driven by reporting copmpleteness. 

While 2025 completeness is generally strong, moderate completeness in some regions suggests that observed disruptions could reflect a mix of service delivery changes and reporting variations."},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.8,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"1d629bfd-e381-4730-a372-5027f629c42f","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"How does indicator completeness affect interpretation of service disruptions for SBA?"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"# Summary of Completeness Trends (July 2023 – June 2025)

Overall completeness: Most maternal and child health indicators (ANC1, deliveries, immunizations, outpatient visits) consistently reported high completeness, generally above 90%.

Areas of weaker completeness: Postnatal care reporting (especially for newborns, ~58–76%) showed persistent gaps, with mothers’ postnatal care also dipping into the mid-80s at times. ANC4 and Penta3 occasionally fell into the 80–89% range, signaling moderate but not severe reporting gaps.

Improvement in 2025: From early 2025, most indicators — including ANC1, deliveries, immunizations, and outpatient visits — sustained or improved to levels above 90%. Postnatal care for newborns showed some recovery but remained the weakest area, suggesting continued caution in interpreting service trends where reporting gaps are likely.

# Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis

Observed values: These are adjusted for outliers only, so they reflect the actual raw service volumes after removing implausible spikes.

Expected values: These are adjusted for both completeness and outliers. This means the model “fills in” where reporting gaps exist, building an expected trend line as if all facilities had reported consistently.

When completeness is high, observed and expected volumes are more comparable, and disruptions are more likely to reflect true service changes.

When completeness is low, expected values may be artificially higher than observed, creating apparent “disruptions” that actually reflect missing reports rather than real declines in service delivery.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.6,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"ecd07d34-206d-4fd5-933e-5957f6c9a5b6","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Completeness is >95% for most indicators in 2025, strengthening confidence in disruption findings"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Service Utilization Assessment","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Purpose: 
Track changes in health service use over time, identifying where services fall below or rise above expected patterns.

# How it works:
- Uses routine HMIS data, cleaned for outliers and missing values.
- Builds an “expected” trend line for each service, adjusting for seasonality and historical trends in service utilization.
- Compares actual service volumes to expected levels.


# Measuring impact:
- Flagged disruption periods are analyzed to estimate how much service volumes changed compared to what was expected.
- Results are shown at nationaland sub-national levels highlighting both system-wide and localized effects.


# How to interpret figures
- Red shaded areas = potential disruptions (service volumes lower than expected).
- Green shaded areas = potential surpluses (service volumes higher than expected).
- These are signals, not conclusions — they highlight when and where volumes deviate, but require further investigation into the underlying reasons (“why”)."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found alongside the source code on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"# Summary of Completeness Trends (July 2023 – June 2025)
Overall completeness: Completeness was generally high across most indicators, with the majority maintaining levels above 85%. This suggests that service-use trends can be interpreted with reasonable confidence as reflecting real changes rather than reporting issues.

Areas of weaker completeness: Completeness remained lower for postnatal care for newborns (often 60–75%) and delivery by cesarean section (around 75–85%). Immunization indicators also showed a modest decline in completeness in the most recent months of 2025.

Improvement in 2025: While levels fluctuated somewhat across indicators, completeness strengthened in early 2025 and remained broadly stable for most services. Despite recent declines for some immunization indicators, overall reporting reliability continued to improve relative to earlier periods.

# Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis

Observed values: These are adjusted for outliers only, so they reflect the actual raw service volumes after removing implausible spikes.

Expected values: These are adjusted for both completeness and outliers. This means the model “fills in” where reporting gaps exist, building an expected trend line as if all facilities had reported consistently.

When completeness is high, observed and expected volumes are more comparable, and disruptions are more likely to reflect true service changes.

When completeness is low, expected values may be artificially higher than observed, creating apparent “disruptions” that actually reflect missing reports rather than real declines in service delivery.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.6,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"ecd07d34-206d-4fd5-933e-5957f6c9a5b6","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Completeness is >95% for most indicators in 2025, strengthening confidence in disruption findings"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Méthodologie : Évaluation de l’utilisation des services","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Objectif :
Suivre l’évolution de l’utilisation des services de santé dans le temps, en identifiant les cas où les services se situent en dessous ou au-dessus des tendances attendues.

# Comment cela fonctionne :
- Utilise les données de routine du DHIS2, nettoyées des valeurs aberrantes et des valeurs manquantes.
- Construit une courbe de tendance « attendue » pour chaque service, ajustée en fonction de la saisonnalité et des tendances historiques d’utilisation des services.
- Compare les volumes de services réels aux niveaux attendus.

# Mesure de l’impact :
- Les périodes signalées comme perturbées sont analysées afin d’estimer l’ampleur de la variation des volumes de services par rapport aux attentes.
-  Les résultats sont présentés aux niveaux national et infranational, mettant en évidence les effets à l’échelle du système et au niveau local.


# Comment interpréter les graphiques
- Zones rouges = perturbations potentielles (volumes de services inférieurs aux attentes).
- Zones vertes = excédents potentiels (volumes de services supérieurs aux attentes).
- Ce sont des signaux, pas des conclusions — ils indiquent quand et où les volumes s’écartent, mais nécessitent des investigations supplémentaires sur les causes (« pourquoi »).
- Les résultats sont ajustés pour la complétude et reflètent de vrais changements en volume des services, et non des variations de rapportage."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Des détails supplémentaires sur la méthodologie et les approches d’ajustement de la qualité des données se trouvent avec le code source sur GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":6,"markdown":"# Résumé des tendances d'exhaustivité 
Aucun indicateur ne présente une complétude forte ; la qualité des données reste variable selon les domaines.
-Intermédiaires : ANC1 (80 %), consultations externes (78 %), accouchements (74 %), BCG (73 %), Penta1 (86 %), Penta3 (86 %), ANC4 (67 %), PNC nouveau-né (65 %).
-Faibles : planification familiale (acceptantes 58 %, conseillés 64 %, méthodes modernes 50–63 %), PNC mères (58 %), allaitement précoce (50 %).
-Vulnérables : nutrition (31 %), petits poids (31 %).
-Critiques : mortalité maternelle et néonatale en formation sanitaire (20–24 %).

# Pourquoi l’exhaustivité est importante pour l’analyse des disruptions

# Approche typique :

Valeurs observées : Ajustées uniquement pour les valeurs aberrantes. Elles reflètent les volumes de services rapportés après suppression des pics non plausibles.
 Valeurs attendues : Ajustées à la fois pour l’exhaustivité et les valeurs aberrantes. Le modèle « comble » les lacunes dues aux rapports manquants afin de générer une ligne de tendance attendue comme si tous les établissements avaient rapporté de façon cohérente.

Lorsque l’exhaustivité est élevée, les valeurs observées et attendues sont comparables, et les disruptions reflètent plus probablement de vrais changements dans l’utilisation des services.

Lorsque l’exhaustivité est faible, les valeurs attendues peuvent sembler artificiellement plus élevées que les valeurs observées, créant des « disruptions » qui reflètent en réalité des lacunes de rapportage plutôt que de véritables baisses de services.

# Notre approche au Cameroun : 

Étant donné que l’exhaustivité est particulièrement faible, nous avons ajusté à la fois les valeurs observées et attendues pour les valeurs aberrantes et l’exhaustivité. Cela rend les résultats plus représentatifs des changements réels de volume de services plutôt que des manques de rapportage. Une limite est que certains mois avec zéro service réel ont pu être imputés, puisque les zéros ne sont pas enregistrés dans les données. Cela signifie que nous avons peut-être, involontairement, corrigé certains cas de véritable absence de prestation de services. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Service Utilization Assessment","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Purpose: 
Track changes in health service use over time, identifying where services fall below or rise above expected patterns.

# How it works:
- Uses routine HMIS data, cleaned for outliers and missing values.
- Builds an “expected” trend line for each service, adjusting for seasonality and historical trends in service utilization.
- Compares actual service volumes to expected levels.


# Measuring impact:
- Flagged disruption periods are analyzed to estimate how much service volumes changed compared to what was expected.
- Results are shown at nationaland sub-national levels highlighting both system-wide and localized effects.


# How to interpret figures
- Red shaded areas = potential disruptions (service volumes lower than expected).
- Green shaded areas = potential surpluses (service volumes higher than expected).
- These are signals, not conclusions — they highlight when and where volumes deviate, but require further investigation into the underlying reasons (“why”)."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found alongside the source code on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"span":4,"markdown":"Reporting completeness for PNC1 for newborns in 2025 is low, with little improvement across years.

Among regions with observed disruptions (Bakool, Bari, Bay, Benadir, Galgadud, Gedo, Hiiraan, Karkaar, Lower Juba, Lower Shabelle, Middle Shebelle, Mudug (Galmudug), Mudug (Puntland), Nugaal, Sanaag, and Sool):

- Low completeness (<80%) was observed in Bakool, Bari, Bay, Benadir, Gedo, Karkaar, Lower Juba, Lower Shabelle, Middle Shebelle, Mudug (Galmudug), Mudug (Puntland), Sanaag, and Sool which may partially contribute to the apparent disruptions there.

- Moderate completeness (80–90%) occurred in Galgadud and Hiiraan, indicating some fluctuations that could slightly influence interpretation of disruptions in those regions.

- Nugaal maintained consistently high completeness (≥90%), suggesting that disruptions observed there are unlikely to be driven by reporting completeness. 

Overall, completeness is generally low, suggesting that observed disruptions could reflect reporting variations.

Nine regions (Ayn, Bakool, Bari, Bay, Benadir, Gedo, Hiiraan, Mudug (Galmudug), and Mudug (Puntland)) experienced some degree of disruption in SBA service delivery, with reported volumes falling below expected levels for at least part of the year.

# Surpluses: 
Three regions (Lower Shabelle, Middle Shebelle, and Sool) recorded periods where reported SBA volumes exceeded expected levels.

# Stable trends:

","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Disruptions:  
Fourteen regions (Ayn, Bari, Bay, Benadir, Gedo, Hiiraan, Karkaar, Lower Juba, Lower Shabelle, Middle Shebelle, Mudug (Galmudug), Mudug (Puntland), Ras-Asayr, and Sool) experienced some degree of disruption in outpatient service delivery, with reported volumes falling below expected levels for at least part of the year.  

# Surpluses:  
Five regions (Ayn, Galgadud, Middle Shebelle, Nugaal, and Sool) recorded periods where reported outpatient volumes exceeded expected levels.  

# Stable trends:  
Two regions (Bakool and Sanaag) showed service volumes broadly aligned with expected trends, suggesting relative stability in outpatient visits.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators — using monthly, facility-level HMIS data.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness.

# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found in the Annex. All R code and source documentation is also publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"span":4,"markdown":"Reporting completeness for ANC1 indicators in 2025 is generally high across most regions, showing improvement compared to earlier years. 

Among regions with observed disruptions (Ayn, Bakool, Bari, Bay, Galgadud, Gedo, Hiiraan, Karkaar, Lower Juba, Lower Shabelle, Middle Shebelle, Mudug (Galmudug), Mudug (Puntland), Ras-Asayr, Sanaag, and Sool):

- Low completeness (<80%) was observed in Ayn and Middle Shebelle, which may partially contribute to the apparent disruptions there.

- Moderate completeness (80–90%) occurred in Bay, Gedo, Hiiraan, Lower Juba, and Lower Shabelle indicating some fluctuations that could slightly influence interpretation of disruptions in those regions.

- The remaining regions maintained consistently high completeness (≥90%), suggesting that disruptions observed there are unlikely to be driven by reporting copmpleteness.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"span":4,"markdown":"Reporting completeness for ANC4 indicators in 2025 is moderate to low across many regions, reflecting persistent reporting challenges over time.

Among regions with observed disruptions (Ayn, Bakool, Bari, Bay, Benadir, Galgadud, Gedo, Hiiraan, Karkaar, Lower Juba, Lower Shabelle, Mudug (Galmudug), Mudug (Puntland), Nugaal, Ras-Asayr, Sanaag, and Sool):

- Low completeness (<80%) was observed in Ayn, Gedo, Lower Juba, Middle Shebelle, Sanaag, and Sool which may partially contribute to the apparent disruptions there.

- Moderate completeness (80–90%) occurred in Bay, Benadir, Galgadud, Hiiraan, Karkaar, Lower Shabelle, and Mudug (Puntland) indicating some fluctuations that could slightly influence interpretation of disruptions in those regions.

- The remaining regions maintained consistently high completeness (≥90%), suggesting that disruptions observed there are unlikely to be driven by reporting completeness.

- Low completeness (<80%) was observed in Ayn, Gedo, Karkaar, Lower Juba, Middle Shebelle, Nugaal, and Ras-Asayr which may partially contribute to the apparent disruptions there.

- Moderate completeness (80–90%) occurred in Bakool, Bay, Hiiraan, Lower Shabelle, Mudug (Galmudug), and Mudug (Puntland) indicating some fluctuations that could slightly influence interpretation of disruptions in those regions.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Analysis of routine health management information systems data (HMIS) and the methodological choices contained in this report provide strengths and limitations which are important to consider in the interpretation of the results."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Strengths 
- Leverages routinely collected HMIS data, enabling timely and low-cost monitoring.
- Includes rigorous data quality assessment and adjustment to improve reliability of estimates.
- Enables subnational analysis for granular insights.
- Supports policy-relevant questions through tailored indicator selection and flexible stratification.
- Accounts for seasonality and historical trends using statistical methods not available in DHIS2.
- Complements existing DHIS2 tools and strengthens data use through feedback loops."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Limitations
- Cannot correct for all types of data quality issues (e.g., systematic underreporting, denominator inaccuracies).
- Does not account for facilities that never report, which may bias results in areas with large reporting gaps.
- Low-volume indicators are more sensitive to anomalies and may not be adjusted.
- Requires consistent indicator definitions and stable reporting systems over time for trend analysis."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Strengths and limitations of this analysis"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"span":4,"markdown":"Reporting completeness for PNC1 for mothers in 2025 is low across many regions, with limited improvement compared to earlier years.

Among regions with observed disruptions (Ayn, Bari, Bay, Benadir, Galgadud, Gedo, Hiiraan, Karkaar, Lower Juba, Lower Shabelle, Middle Shebelle, Mudug (Galmudug), Mudug (Puntland), Nugaal, and Sool):

- Low completeness (<80%) was observed in Ayn, Gedo, Lower Juba, Middle Shebelle, Mudug (Galmudug), and Sool which may partially contribute to the apparent disruptions there.

- Moderate completeness (80–90%) occurred in Bay, Benadir, Karkaar, and Lower Shabelle indicating some fluctuations that could slightly influence interpretation of disruptions in those regions.

- The remaining regions maintained consistently high completeness (≥90%), suggesting that disruptions observed there are unlikely to be driven by reporting completeness. 

"},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.8,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"6a7804f5-8f77-432c-b89e-21564fcef8a7","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":"pnc1_mother","replicateBy":"indicator_common_id"}}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"How does indicator completeness affect interpretation of service disruptions for PNC1 (mothers)?"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# What is data quality assessment?
The Data Quality Assessment (DQA) methodology applies three core assessments: detection of extreme outliers in reported service volumes, analysis of indicator-specific completeness, and consistency checks between related indicators. For each facility and month, the data are processed to identify anomalies, assess reporting continuity, and validate internal coherence across indicators. Outliers are flagged using Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) methods and proportional thresholds. Completeness is evaluated based on whether facilities report non-zero values across months for core indicators. Consistency is checked across linked indicators (e.g., ANC1 vs ANC4, Penta1 vs Penta3), using predefined ratio thresholds. The results are integrated into a composite DQA score that summarizes whether a facility's data meet all quality benchmarks.

# What is data quality adjustment? 
Data quality adjustment is the process of correcting known issues in routine health data to improve the reliability of analyses. In the FASTR approach, adjustments are applied to HMIS data using automated methods that identify and correct outlier values and impute missing data based on patterns in historical reporting. This allows countries to make more robust use of the data they already have, even when reporting is imperfect.

# Why adjust for data quality?
Routine health data, such as those captured in DHIS2, are an essential resource for monitoring and improving primary health care systems. However, data quality issues — like outliers, incomplete reporting, and inconsistencies between related indicators — can distort analyses and misinform decision-making. 
The FASTR approach includes built-in statistical methods to identify and adjust for these issues, producing more robust and actionable results. While not all data quality challenges can be fully resolved, targeted adjustments improve the reliability of the data and help ensure it can still inform decisions. Rather than treating poor data quality as a barrier, FASTR transforms it into an opportunity for feedback and improvement, enabling countries to use the data they have to make timely, evidence-based decisions.

# How was adjustment done?
The Data Quality Adjustment module uses HMIS data together with the outlier and completeness flags generated by the Data Quality Assessment module, which identify where service volumes are unusually high or missing. Outlier values are replaced with a centered 12-month facility-level rolling average based on valid (non-flagged) data. Missing values due to incomplete reporting are imputed using the same method. If there is not enough valid historical data, a fallback average at the facility level is used instead. Adjustments are applied under four scenarios - no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and both combined - to enable sensitivity analysis. Indicators which don't meet minimum volume thresholds are excluded from adjustment.
For this analysis, adjustment is for outliers and completeness."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology: Data Quality Adjustment Approach"}}
